It says these large companies have [zh:吸引] 'attracted' a lot of [zh:人材] 'talent' from [zh:世界各地] 'all over the world'. This group includes Chinese students who studied [zh:在国外] 'abroad' and graduated. The word [zh:留学生] specifically means 'students studying abroad' or 'overseas students'.

content.focus_text: 这些大公司吸引了世界各地不少的人材，包括一些在国外学习毕业的中国留学生。
content.focus_pinyin: Zhèxiē dà gōngsī xīyǐnle shìjiè gèdì bùshǎo de réncái, bāokuò yīxiē zài guówài xuéxí bìyè de Zhōngguó liúxuéshēng.

segments.segment_title: Vocabulary: 人材 vs. 人才
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the interchangeability and subtle difference between 人材 and 人才.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the word for 'talent'. In the text, you see [zh:人材]. You might also see it written as [zh:人才]. For all practical purposes, they are interchangeable. The character [zh:材] literally means 'timber' or 'material', suggesting a person is a valuable resource for a company or country. The character [zh:才] means 'talent' or 'ability' directly. Both refer to a talented person.

content.focus_text: 人材 / 人才
content.focus_gloss_en: person of ability, talent

highlight_words.explanation_en: A person of ability. Can also be written as 人才.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce and explain the modern Chinese term '海归' (hǎiguī) for overseas returnees.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This line introduces a very important modern Chinese term. It says when these students [zh:归来] 'return' from [zh:海外] 'overseas', they are [zh:被叫做] 'called' [zh:海归]. This word is a clever combination of [zh:海] from 'overseas' and [zh:归] from 'return'. It specifically refers to Chinese people who have returned to China after studying or working abroad.

content.focus_text: 他们从海外归来，被叫做“海归”。
content.focus_pinyin: Tāmen cóng hǎiwài guīlái, bèi jiàozuò “hǎiguī”.

A formal way to say 'to come back' or 'to return'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now for a bit of Chinese humor. The text says some people jokingly call them [zh:海龟]. Why? Because the word for 'sea turtle', [zh:海龟], is pronounced exactly the same as the word for 'returnee', [zh:海归]. This is a pun, a common and fun aspect of the Chinese language. The phrase [zh:开玩笑] means 'to joke' or 'to kid'.

content.focus_text: 有的人还开玩笑地把他们叫做“海龟”。

A common phrase meaning to make a joke or to kid around.
A pun on '海归' (returnee) because they have the same pronunciation.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 海归 vs. 海龟
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural context of the '海龟' pun and its opposite, '土鳖'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's dive deeper into this pun. [zh:海归] refers to a highly educated person returning from overseas. The pun [zh:海龟], meaning 'sea turtle', is a funny and widely used nickname. There's even an opposite term: [zh:土鳖], which literally means 'rustic soft-shelled turtle'. People who have never studied abroad might use this term to describe themselves in a self-deprecating way. It's a great example of modern Chinese slang.

content.focus_text: 海归 (hǎiguī) vs. 海龟 (hǎiguī)
content.focus_gloss_en: Returnee vs. Sea Turtle (Pun)
content.notes: Opposite: 土鳖 (tǔbiē) - 'rustic turtle', someone who hasn't been abroad.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A person who has returned from overseas, typically after studying.
A pun on '海归' due to identical pronunciation.
Literally 'soft-shelled turtle', a self-deprecating slang term for someone who has never studied abroad, the opposite of a '海归'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person with a graduation cap and suitcase (海归) next to an icon of a sea turtle (海龟). Then introduce the '土鳖' icon, perhaps a turtle staying on land.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce the main character, Lin Xuemei, and understand how the 'sea turtle' pun is applied to her.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here we meet our main character. The sentence says, 'Lin Xuemei is now one of these new sea turtles.' Notice the measure word used here: [zh:一只]. This measure word is typically used for animals. Using it here with [zh:海龟] reinforces the 'sea turtle' pun and adds to the playful tone of the description.

content.focus_text: 林雪梅现在就是其中的一只新“海龟”。
content.focus_pinyin: Lín Xuěméi xiànzài jiùshì qízhōng de yì zhī xīn “hǎiguī”.
content.focus_gloss_en: Lin Xuemei is now one of these new 'sea turtles'.

A measure word, often used for animals, which playfully reinforces the 'sea turtle' pun.

segments.teaching_goal: To describe Lin Xuemei's actions after returning to China and learn vocabulary related to job searching.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence describes what Xuemei did after coming back to China. [zh:以后不久] means 'not long after'. So, not long after returning, she immediately started her job search. The sentence lists three key actions: [zh:上网] 'to go online', [zh:查资料] 'to search for information', and [zh:找工作] 'to look for a job'.

content.focus_text: 雪梅回到中国以后不久,就开始上网查资料、找工作。
content.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi huídào Zhōngguó yǐhòu bùjiǔ, jiù kāishǐ shàngwǎng chá zīliào, zhǎo gōngzuò.
content.focus_gloss_en: Not long after returning to China, Xuemei started searching for information and looking for a job online.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain Xuemei's past internship experience and her feelings about it.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This line tells us about her initial job-seeking experience. She had interned at several companies, which is [zh:实习]. The particle [zh:过] after the verb indicates a past experience. However, the sentence says she [zh:都不太满意], meaning she 'wasn't very satisfied with any of them'. The key word here is [zh:满意], which means 'satisfied'.

content.focus_text: 她去过几家公司实习,但是都不太满意。
content.focus_pinyin: Tā qùguo jǐ jiā gōngsī shíxí, dànshì dōu bú tài mǎnyì.
content.focus_gloss_en: She had interned at several companies, but wasn't very satisfied with any of them.

highlight_words.explanation_en: To work as an intern; an internship.
An adjective or verb meaning to be pleased or satisfied. Here it's negated with [zh:不太] to mean 'not very satisfied'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the details of the job interview opportunity Xuemei received.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence is a bit long, but it contains the key event. Let's break it down. 'Yesterday, a large company that she really likes...' ([zh:昨天有一家她非常喜欢的大公司]). '...asked her to come in this morning for an interview...' ([zh:叫她今天上午去面试]). The job is for a [zh:销售经理] 'sales manager'. The word [zh:面试] is 'job interview', a very important word for this lesson.

content.focus_text: 昨天有一家她非常喜欢的大公司叫她今天上午去面试销售经理的工作。
content.focus_pinyin: Zuótiān yǒu yī jiā tā fēicháng xǐhuān de dà gōngsī jiào tā jīntiān shàngwǔ qù miànshì xiāoshòu jīnglǐ de gōngzuò.
content.focus_gloss_en: Yesterday, a large company that she really likes asked her to come in this morning for an interview for a sales manager position.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'face test', this is the word for a job interview.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence sets the timing for the phone call. [zh:面试以后] means 'after the interview'. The structure [zh:刚...就...] is very useful. It means 'as soon as... then...'. So, 'as soon as she got home, she received a call from Lisa'. The verb phrase [zh:接到电话] means 'to receive a phone call'.

content.focus_text: 面试以后,刚回到家里就接到丽莎的电话。
content.focus_pinyin: Miànshì yǐhòu, gāng huídào jiālǐ jiù jiēdào Lìshā de diànhuà.
content.focus_gloss_en: After the interview, she had just gotten home when she received a call from Lisa.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A structure indicating that the second action happens immediately after the first.
Means to receive something, like a call or a letter. [zh:接] is 'to connect' and [zh:到] indicates successful completion.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand Lisa's opening question and the use of '怎么样'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here the dialogue begins. Lisa starts with [zh:哎], a casual interjection like 'Hey!'. She playfully calls Xuemei '[zh:海龟]小姐', or 'Ms. Sea Turtle'. Then she asks the main question: [zh:今天的面试怎么样?], which means 'How was your interview today?'. The phrase [zh:怎么样] is a very common and useful way to ask for an opinion or to ask how something went.

content.focus_text: 哎,“海龟”小姐,今天的面试怎么样?
content.focus_pinyin: Āi, “hǎiguī” xiǎojiě, jīntiān de miànshì zěnmeyàng?
content.focus_gloss_en: Hey, Ms. 'Sea Turtle,' how was your interview today?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An interjection used to get someone's attention, similar to 'Hey' or 'Oh'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe feeling nervous and sweating.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The speaker starts with 'Ha,' an exclamation of relief. She says [zh:开始有些紧张], meaning 'at the beginning, I was a bit nervous.' Then, [zh:出了好多汗], 'I sweated a lot.' The result? [zh:套装的上衣都快湿了] - 'The top of my suit was almost soaked.' Let's look at the key words: [zh:紧张] is 'nervous,' [zh:汗] is 'sweat,' and [zh:湿] means 'wet.'

content.focus_text: 哈,开始有些紧张,出了好多汗,套装的上衣都快湿了。
content.focus_pinyin: Hā, kāishǐ yǒuxiē jǐnzhāng, chūle hǎoduō hàn, tàozhuāng de shàngyī dōu kuài shī le.
content.focus_gloss_en: Ha, I was a bit nervous at the beginning. I sweated so much that the top of my suit was almost soaked.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Describes a feeling of anxiety or tension.
The noun for perspiration. 'To sweat' is [zh:出汗].
A set of matching clothes, like a business suit.
Describes a feeling of anxiety or tension.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask 'why' informally and reference past experiences.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Her friend asks a reasonable question. She starts with [zh:你已经不是第一次面试了], 'This isn't your first interview.' The phrase [zh:已经不是第一次] is very useful for talking about repeated experiences. Then she asks, [zh:干吗那么紧张], 'Why were you so nervous?' [zh:干吗] is an informal way to say 'why,' similar to 'what for?'

content.focus_text: 你已经不是第一次面试了,干吗那么紧张?
content.focus_pinyin: Nǐ yǐjīng búshì dì yī cì miànshì le, gànmá nàme jǐnzhāng?
content.focus_gloss_en: This isn't your first interview. Why were you so nervous?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates that an action or state has already occurred or been reached.
An informal interrogative used in spoken language to ask for the reason.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe a person's role and demeanor.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here's the reason for her nervousness. She explains, [zh:给我面试的是他们公司的总经理]. This means, 'The person who interviewed me was their company's general manager.' The key title here is [zh:总经理]. She adds that he was [zh:挺严肃的], which means 'very stern' or 'serious.' The word [zh:挺] is a common adverb for 'very'.

content.focus_text: 给我面试的是他们公司的总经理,挺严肃的,
content.focus_pinyin: Gěi wǒ miànshì de shì tāmen gōngsī de zǒngjīnglǐ, tǐng yánsù de,
content.focus_gloss_en: The person who interviewed me was their general manager, he was very stern,

highlight_words.explanation_en: The head of a company. [zh:总] means 'general' or 'chief', and [zh:经理] is 'manager'.
Describes a person who is not smiling and has a formal, strict demeanor.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a challenging interview question and its underlying assumption.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The manager's first sentence was very direct and challenging. [zh:第一句话就是] means 'His first sentence was...'. He asks, [zh:你是不是在美国找不到工作才回中国来的]. This question implies a skeptical assumption: 'Is it because you couldn't find a job in the US that you came back to China?' The structure [zh:是不是...才...] is often used to voice such a suspicion.

content.focus_text: 第一句话就是:“你是不是在美国找不到工作才回中国来的?”
content.focus_pinyin: dì yī jù huà jiùshì: “Nǐ shìbushì zài Měiguó zhǎobudào gōngzuò cái huí Zhōngguó lái de?”
content.focus_gloss_en: His first sentence was: 'Did you come back to China because you couldn't find a job in the United States?'

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express empathy using 'no wonder'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The friend completely understands the nervousness now. She exclaims, [zh:这个总经理可真的挺吓人的]! 'This general manager is really scary!' The word [zh:吓人] literally means 'to scare people.' Then she says, [zh:难怪你紧张], 'No wonder you were nervous.' [zh:难怪] is a great word to use when you understand the reason for something that was previously puzzling.

content.focus_text: 这个总经理可真的挺吓人的!难怪你紧张。
content.focus_pinyin: Zhège zǒngjīnglǐ kě zhēnde tǐng xiàrén de! Nánguài nǐ jǐnzhāng.
content.focus_gloss_en: This general manager is really scary! No wonder you were nervous.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the '越...越...' structure for correlated changes.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The speaker describes a downward spiral. She says, [zh:我越紧张就越解释不清楚]. This uses the important [zh:越...越...] structure, meaning 'the more... the more...'. So, 'The more nervous I got, the more unclear my explanation became.' The verb [zh:解释] means 'to explain,' and [zh:不清楚] means 'not clear' or 'incoherent.'

content.focus_pinyin: Wǒ yuè jǐnzhāng jiù yuè jiěshì bù qīngchu.
content.focus_gloss_en: The more nervous I got, the less clearly I explained myself.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Then, she had a breakthrough. [zh:后来我突然想出了一个主意]. 'Later, I suddenly had an idea.' [zh:突然] means 'suddenly,' and [zh:主意] is 'idea.' What was the idea? [zh:什么也不说了], 'I stopped talking,' and instead, she took action: [zh:从包里拿出两张纸,放到他前面], 'took out two pieces of paper from my bag, and placed them in front of him.'

content.focus_pinyin: Hòulái wǒ tūrán xiǎngchūle yí ge zhǔyi, shénme yě bù shuō le, cóng bāo li náchū liǎng zhāng zhǐ, fàngdào tā qiánmiàn.
content.focus_gloss_en: Later, I suddenly had an idea. I stopped talking, took out two pieces of paper from my bag, and placed them in front of him.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the term for 'job offer letter'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here's the reveal of her secret weapon. She explains they were [zh:录用通知] - 'job offer letters.' This is a crucial term for job hunting. [zh:录用] means 'to hire' or 'to accept for employment,' and [zh:通知] means 'notice.' So, a 'hiring notice.' These letters were from two companies in California, sent in May.

content.focus_text: 是加州的两家公司五月寄给我的录用通知。

segments.teaching_goal: To understand questions starting with '既然' (since).

narration_track.subtitle_en: The manager sees the proof and his question changes. [zh:总经理看了接着问] - 'After the general manager looked at them, he then asked...'. His new question starts with [zh:既然]. This word means 'since' or 'given that,' and it introduces a premise that is now known to both parties. He asks, [zh:既然你...找到了工作, 为什么还要...], 'Since you've already found a job..., why do you still want to...?'

content.focus_text: 总经理看了接着问:“既然你在美国公司找到了工作,为什么还要回中国工作呢?”

content.focus_gloss_en: After the general manager looked at them, he then asked, 'Since you've already found a job with an American company, why do you still want to come back to work in China?'

narration_track.subtitle_en: The friend thinks the answer is simple. She says, [zh:这个问题不难回答], 'That question isn't hard to answer.' Her reason? [zh:你的男朋友在中国留学嘛]! This 'Verb-ing, Verb-ing' structure suggests that while an action was in progress, something else happened. What happened? [zh:脸上多云转晴了]. His face changed 'from partly cloudy to sunny.' segments.teaching_goal: To understand and differentiate two common weather metaphors used to describe changes in facial expressions.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the two creative phrases from this dialogue. Both are borrowed from weather forecasts. First, [zh:阴转多云], literally 'overcast turning to partly cloudy.' This describes a slight improvement in someone's mood, like when a stern face softens. Second, [zh:多云转晴], 'partly cloudy turning to sunny.' This indicates a much bigger, positive change, like when a neutral face breaks into a happy smile. They are a humorous and vivid way to talk about emotions.

The question he asks is a classic one in job interviews: [zh:你觉得你自己最大的缺点是什么], which translates to 'What do you think is your biggest weakness?'. segments.segment_title: Vocabulary: 零食 vs. 小吃
segments.teaching_goal: To differentiate between 'língshí' (packaged snacks) and 'xiǎochī' (light meals/street food).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the word [zh:零食]. It's important to distinguish it from another word, [zh:小吃]. [zh:零食] refers to pre-packaged snacks like candies or chips that you'd buy in a store. On the other hand, [zh:小吃] refers to light, informal meals or street food, often made fresh and associated with a specific region. So, while both can be translated as 'snack', they are not interchangeable.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the '越...越...' (yuè...yuè...) structure to express that one thing changes in accordance with another.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now, let's dive into our first grammar point: the structure [zh:越] X [zh:越] Y. This pattern means 'the more X, the more Y.' It shows that the degree of Y changes along with the degree of X. In the dialogue, Xuemei said, [zh:我越紧张就越解释不清楚]. This means 'The more nervous I got, the less clearly I could explain.' Notice how her nervousness directly impacted her ability to speak clearly. Here's another example: [zh:你越说我越不懂], which means 'The more you explain, the less I understand.' This is a very useful pattern to describe escalating situations or feelings.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the use of '既然' (jìrán) to introduce a known fact or premise, followed by a logical conclusion or question.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next up is the conjunction [zh:既然]. We use [zh:既然] to start a sentence when we want to state a reason or premise that is already known to both the speaker and the listener. The second part of the sentence then presents a logical conclusion or question based on that premise. For instance, the manager asked Xuemei, [zh:既然你在美国公司找到了工作,为什么还要回中国工作呢]. This translates to 'Since you've already found a job with an American company, why do you still want to come back to work in China?' The manager is using the known fact of her US job offer to ask a logical follow-up question. Remember, while it indicates a reason, it's different from [zh:因为] because [zh:既然] emphasizes a shared understanding of the premise.

content.focus_text: 既然 + Premise, + Conclusion
content.focus_pinyin: jìrán + Premise, + Conclusion
content.focus_gloss_en: Since..., ...

grammar_points.pattern: 既然你在美国公司找到了工作,为什么还要回中国工作呢?
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Jìrán nǐ zài Měiguó gōngsī zhǎodào le gōngzuò, wèishéme hái yào huí Zhōngguó gōngzuò ne?
grammar_points.explanation_en: Since you've already found a job with an American company, why do you still want to come back to work in China?

content.notes: The premise is a fact already known to the listener.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to introduce a premise that is already known, leading to a logical conclusion or inference.

segments.visual_notes: Use a 'lightbulb' icon appearing over the speaker's head to signify the known premise, followed by a question mark or an arrow pointing to the conclusion.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the 'Verb + 着 + Verb + 着' pattern, which indicates an unexpected event occurring during an ongoing action.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is a dynamic one: 'Verb [zh:着] Verb [zh:着]'. This structure describes an action that is ongoing, and then, surprisingly or unintentionally, a second action happens. In our story, Xuemei says the manager [zh:听着听着,脸上多云转晴了]. This means 'As the general manager listened and listened, his gloomy face gradually brightened up.' The listening was continuous, and the change in his expression was the surprising result. The adverb [zh:就] is often used before the second verb. For example, [zh:那个孩子哭着哭着就睡着了] means 'The child cried and cried and then fell asleep.' The sleeping was an unintended consequence of the crying.

content.focus_text: Verb + 着 + Verb + 着, (就) + Unintended Result
content.focus_pinyin: Verb + zhe + Verb + zhe, (jiù) + Unintended Result
content.focus_gloss_en: While doing something, something else happens unexpectedly.

grammar_points.pattern: 总经理听着听着,脸上多云转晴了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Zǒngjīnglǐ tīngzhe tīngzhe, liǎnshang duōyún zhuǎn qíng le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: As the general manager listened, his gloomy face gradually brightened up.

grammar_points.explanation_en: The child cried and cried and (then) fell asleep.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A pattern indicating that while an action is ongoing, a second, often surprising, action occurs.

segments.visual_notes: Show a continuous action (like listening) with a looping animation, then have a surprise element (like a smiley face appearing) pop up.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar patterns and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! We've covered some very useful grammar today. We learned how to say 'the more, the more' with [zh:越...越...], how to form conclusions based on known facts with [zh:既然], and how to describe surprising events with 'Verb [zh:着] Verb [zh:着]'. Keep practicing these patterns, and you'll sound more and more fluent. See you next time!

items.hanzi: 21世纪初以来,中国的经济有了很大的发展,不少跨国公司进入中国。
items.source_text: 21世纪初以来,中国的经济有了很大的发展,不少跨国公司进入中国。

items.hanzi: 这些大公司吸引了世界各地不少的人材，包括一些在国外学习毕业的中国留学生。
items.source_text: 这些大公司吸引了世界各地不少的人材，包括一些在国外学习毕业的中国留学生。

items.hanzi: 他们从海外归来，被叫做“海归”。
items.source_text: 他们从海外归来，被叫做“海归”。

items.hanzi: 有的人还开玩笑地把他们叫做“海龟”。
items.source_text: 有的人还开玩笑地把他们叫做“海龟”。

items.hanzi: 林雪梅现在就是其中的一只新“海龟”。
items.source_text: 林雪梅现在就是其中的一只新“海龟”。

items.hanzi: 雪梅回到中国以后不久,就开始上网查资料、找工作。
items.source_text: 雪梅回到中国以后不久,就开始上网查资料、找工作。

items.hanzi: 她去过几家公司实习,但是都不太满意。
items.source_text: 她去过几家公司实习,但是都不太满意。

items.hanzi: 昨天有一家她非常喜欢的大公司叫她今天上午去面试销售经理的工作。
items.source_text: 昨天有一家她非常喜欢的大公司叫她今天上午去面试销售经理的工作。

items.hanzi: 面试以后,刚回到家里就接到丽莎的电话。
items.source_text: 面试以后,刚回到家里就接到丽莎的电话。

items.hanzi: 哎,“海龟”小姐,今天的面试怎么样?
items.source_text: 哎,“海龟”小姐,今天的面试怎么样?

items.hanzi: 哈,开始有些紧张,出了好多汗,套装的上衣都快湿了。
items.source_text: 哈,开始有些紧张,出了好多汗,套装的上衣都快湿了。

items.hanzi: 你已经不是第一次面试了,干吗那么紧张?
items.source_text: 你已经不是第一次面试了,干吗那么紧张?

items.hanzi: 给我面试的是他们公司的总经理,挺严肃的,第一句话就是:“你是不是在美国找不到工作才回中国来的?”
items.source_text: 给我面试的是他们公司的总经理,挺严肃的,第一句话就是:“你是不是在美国找不到工作才回中国来的?”

items.hanzi: 这个总经理可真的挺吓人的!难怪你紧张。
items.source_text: 这个总经理可真的挺吓人的!难怪你紧张。

items.hanzi: 我越紧张就越解释不清楚。后来我突然想出了一个主意,什么也不说了,从包里拿出两张纸,放到他前面。他一看,脸上马上阴转多云了。
items.source_text: 我越紧张就越解释不清楚。后来我突然想出了一个主意,什么也不说了,从包里拿出两张纸,放到他前面。他一看,脸上马上阴转多云了。

items.hanzi: 什么纸?我也去买两张准备着。
items.source_text: 什么纸?我也去买两张准备着。

items.hanzi: 是加州的两家公司五月寄给我的录用通知。总经理看了接着问:“既然你在美国公司找到了工作,为什么还要回中国工作呢?”
items.source_text: 是加州的两家公司五月寄给我的录用通知。总经理看了接着问:“既然你在美国公司找到了工作,为什么还要回中国工作呢?”

items.hanzi: 这个问题不难回答,你的男朋友在中国留学嘛!
items.source_text: 这个问题不难回答,你的男朋友在中国留学嘛!

items.hanzi: 我要是这样说,他很可能会以为我在中国找工作,只是短期打算。好在这个时候我已经不紧张了。我就从中国经济发展,谈到他们公司的产品,再谈产品的销售,然后解释为什么希望去他们公司工作。总经理听着听着,脸上多云转晴了。
items.source_text: 我要是这样说,他很可能会以为我在中国找工作,只是短期打算。好在这个时候我已经不紧张了。我就从中国经济发展,谈到他们公司的产品,再谈产品的销售,然后解释为什么希望去他们公司工作。总经理听着听着,脸上多云转晴了。

items.hanzi: 面试完了吧?
items.source_text: 面试完了吧?

items.hanzi: 还没呢!这时候总经理突然笑着问:“你觉得你自己最大的缺点是什么?”
items.source_text: 还没呢!这时候总经理突然笑着问:“你觉得你自己最大的缺点是什么?”

items.hanzi: 哈,我觉得,你最大的缺点就是爱吃零食。
items.source_text: 哈,我觉得,你最大的缺点就是爱吃零食。

items.hanzi: 那他不是要把我看成小女孩了?
items.source_text: 那他不是要把我看成小女孩了?

items.hanzi: 那你是怎么回答的?
items.source_text: 那你是怎么回答的?

items.hanzi: 我说,一个好的销售管理人材,应该能科学地安排自己的时间,善于工作的人往往也是善于休息的,我在这方面还很不够。实习的时候,我工作一有压力就熬夜加班。所以,不太会休息可能是我最大的缺点。
items.source_text: 我说,一个好的销售管理人材,应该能科学地安排自己的时间,善于工作的人往往也是善于休息的,我在这方面还很不够。实习的时候,我工作一有压力就熬夜加班。所以,不太会休息可能是我最大的缺点。

items.hanzi: 雪梅,你到底是在说自己的缺点还是优点啊?
items.source_text: 雪梅,你到底是在说自己的缺点还是优点啊?

items.hanzi: 我又说,只要我们能把缺点变成优点,缺点就没那么可怕了。说到这儿,总经理站起来,一边跟我握手,一边笑着说,他相信我的能力,让我等他们的好消息。
items.source_text: 我又说,只要我们能把缺点变成优点,缺点就没那么可怕了。说到这儿,总经理站起来,一边跟我握手,一边笑着说,他相信我的能力,让我等他们的好消息。

items.hanzi: 太棒了!恭喜你,林经理!
items.source_text: 太棒了!恭喜你,林经理!
